title: X will now block Grok users from editing people into bikinis - Music Ally
text:
X has
announced
that it will no longer allow people to use its Grok AI to turn photos of real people into bikini shots without their permission.
“We now geoblock the ability of all users to generate images of real people in bikinis, underwear, and similar attire via the Grok account and in Grok in X in those jurisdictions where it’s illegal,” added the company in its announcement.
Oh, and: “The rapid evolution of generative AI presents challenges across the entire industry. We are actively working with users, our partners, governing bodies and other platforms to address issues more rapidly as they arise.”
It’s more accurate to say that governing bodies are forcing X (and its parent company xAI) to deal with this issue. The latest to take an interest is California attorney general Rob Bonta, who has
launched an investigation
into “the proliferation of nonconsensual sexually explicit material produced using Grok”.
“xAI appears to be facilitating the large-scale production of deepfake nonconsensual intimate images that are being used to harass women and girls across the internet, including via the social media platform X,” added his announcement.
As for the ‘partners’ mentioned in X’s post, they’re under pressure too. A coalition of women’s groups and technology activists yesterday
published open letters calling on Apple and Google to remove the X and Grok apps from their stores
.
“We are really imploring Apple and Google to take this extremely seriously,” Jenna Sherman, campaign director of Ultraviolet – one of the organisations involved – told Reuters.
The news organisation also printed xAI’s three-word response when it contacted the company: “Legacy Media Lies”. But the changes to Grok suggest it is at last taking the issue more seriously than this suggests.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
